d_notes.title: Designer's Notes
d_notes.description: This is my personal portfolio website, built from scratch using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

gallery.title: Initial Portfolio Sketch
gallery.description: This sketch shows the first visual idea for the portfolio layout and content structure.

gallery.title: index Variatrions
gallery.description: These early home page sketches explore the structure and flow of the main landing experience. The layout is organized vertically to guide the user through key sections—projects, skills, and contact—while maintaining a strong visual entry point at the top. The hero section was conceived as a billboard-like composition, combining large typography with graphic elements to immediately capture attention. A fixed sidebar was introduced to provide constant navigation access, allowing users to move quickly between sections without losing context.

gallery.title: index & Interaction Concept
gallery.description: The home page was intentionally designed as a section-based layout to improve navigation speed and user flow. Instead of separating content into multiple pages, the goal was to allow users to access key information—projects, skills, and contact—without unnecessary page reloads. This approach reduces friction in the browsing experience. While the initial load may include more content, navigation within the page becomes faster and more intuitive, especially for users who want a quick overview of my work. The Projects section on the home page is treated as a featured preview. It loads only essential visual elements, such as images, to keep performance efficient while still engaging the user. More detailed content is deferred to dedicated project pages. From a visual perspective, the design was inspired by the idea of a billboard—bold, graphic, and attention-grabbing—combined with the structure of a functional website. The intention was to create something that feels expressive and memorable, while still maintaining clarity and usability. Interactive elements, such as cursor-based animations, were added to make the experience more dynamic and to reflect the intersection between design and technology. The goal was not only to present content, but to create a more engaging and distinctive interaction. Although frameworks could achieve similar results, I chose to build this using custom code to maintain full control over layout, behavior, and performance. This approach also allowed me to better understand how design decisions translate into real implementation, reinforcing the connection between visual design and front-end development.

gallery.title: Visual Design & Implementation Flow
gallery.description: The diagram represents the workflow from the initial concept in Figma to the final deployment.

projects.title: J&G Photography
projects.description: Modern, interactive, and customizable website built with HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and Bootstrap. Features include a dynamic navigation menu with scroll-based background color, a glass-effect hamburger menu without affecting carousel images, a custom service package builder using JSON and dropdowns, lightweight FormSubmit integration for messaging, and Google Maps iframe for location. Hosted on Vercel for free deployment, shortened URL, and basic analytics.
captions: Homepage hero with dynamic top menu
Hamburger menu with glass blur effect
Generated message ready to send via FormSubmit
Embedded Google Maps for studio location

projects.title: Handyman DMD
projects.description: Development of a simple and user-friendly website for HandymanDMD...

projects.title: Aurora Wellness Center
projects.description: Aurora Wellness Center is a personal project built on the free version of WordPress. Due to the limitations of the free plan, no custom CSS or HTML could be used, so the site was structured entirely using covers, columns, rows, and careful margin adjustments. The page is designed as a single-page layout with anchor links to different sections, providing a training exercise in organizing content and design with minimal tools. This project focuses on a calm and professional aesthetic, with warm, editorial-style photography, soft colors (greens, beige, cream, golden accents), and clear visual hierarchy. The site is still in development, serving as a practical exercise in layout, structure, and usability within WordPress constraints.

projects.title: Quill Pen Press
projects.description: Quill Pen Press targeted emerging writers who wanted their books professionally bound and promoted after a selection process. The company was new, had few clients, no website, and faced strong competition, making it difficult to gain visibility and credibility. The solution was to create a professional and approachable brand identity using navy blue and gold, a beige website background, and a stylized 'Q' logo with the company name, emphasizing quality and professionalism to build trust and attract potential customers. The website was designed and implemented in Webflow to quickly create a responsive, visually polished site without needing custom coding.
